concept: Legible / Visually or Tactually Distinguishable for Reading
description: Clear enough for a stated reader to distinguish and read under the relevant visual or tactile conditions. Legibility may depend on size, spacing, contrast, surface, script familiarity, or display quality. 'theo' (read) is the reader's act; 'thewuni' is a property of what meets that reader.
sound_symbolism: Fine dental breath carries 'the' into balanced 'e'. Stressed 'wu' rounds the lips and sinks into deep 'u'. Final 'ni' holds the tongue at the teeth in nasal resonance, then rises to bright 'i'. After dropping into a deep center, the sound returns to a small clear point.
grammatical_notes: A predicative or attributive adjective: 'ha thekiro thewuni nai.' (This writing is legible.) The reader and conditions may appear in surrounding clauses. The quality-noun rule supplies legibility without claiming that the text is understood or accurate.

pillars.art-nouveau-aesthetics: Letterform, spacing, surface, and contrast meet their purpose when a reader can follow them rather than merely admire them.
pillars.peace-linguistics-practices: A legibility claim remains answerable to the reader instead of allowing the maker's intention to settle whether the words can be read.

tags.physical: reading conditions involving sight or touch
